speaker: interviewer
text: Lili Frank Grossman. Your--

speaker: interviewee
text: I could talk with you that, like the Seder, up till the morning, more and more experience and all terrible things. I was always working. I know I have to bring home my mother. I trusted we were liberated. Somehow, we are going to live through the whole things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel that when you were doing this death march on foot, when you were walking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Because we were always the first one to go. Because if you were at the end of the group, you were finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the strongest were nearer to the front, usually?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not just the strongest, the more willing to work and surviving. And other people was sick. If I fall sick, or my mother, then you can't do anything against, but you try. The hope hold you together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we get in the-- in the Hitlerjugend camp. And we didn't do anything there. We were there two or three weeks. Finally, at Friday, late afternoon-- Explain what a Hitlerjugend camp is.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a camp. Nothing. Buildings. Barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Young people there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody was there this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was empty.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they already escaped from there. And a very high-ranking guy who was with us [INAUDIBLE] also, but we never-- almost never saw him, made a speech for us from that hill down to us. And he was talking like how nice we were. And we will be freed and everything. I think we are-- I think we are in the death row, or he is drunk. But we didn't realize many things. We lost all our senses. Everything for normal life, everything we lost. No time, no feeling. People was cutting frozen horse on the road when we were death march and eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Raw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Raw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just eating it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what? You won't believe it. Even mice when they got. Anyway, so we started to walking at night. Just-- they just-- just let us to walk, but not just like a brisk walk, you know, going. But we heard already the canon and the Stalin candle. You know what is that? They throw down and light up everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a flare.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a flare. But you know what? That was terrible, lighted up right and left and everywhere. We were just praying not to on us. And then that-- the airplane's coming. And they, you know, throw down the bombs. That is why they need it for, is the Stalin candles.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we get in a village at morning, Saturday morning. Friday night, we started, Saturday morning. And we went again a big barn. They closed the door on us, and we heard the gunfight all over. And then some Russian soldier came over there, because we were screaming this time. We don't know-- we didn't know we are liberated, OK? We didn't know that is the end. We are going to die. And opened up the doors-- the gates, not doors-- gates. And he said in Russian, you are free. And we didn't move. We just stayed there, frozen. We-- it needs 10 minutes until we got to sense. So we went out. All over was Russian soldier, Russian tanks all over. And what do you think? The guard was all over, shot, our guard. They deserved that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But most of them run to their houses, and they were asking civil clothes. But we showed them to the Russian. And our oberscharfuhrer Meier, the tank went through on him. And he was-- he were like a [? crepe. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I saw. I went and I put my feet the middle of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stomped on him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was dancing on him. We were animals, OK? Don't misunderstood me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were animals. So we went the first house and started to eat. But that was many peoples that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because they overate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. We went in a, we called a [NON-ENGLISH], where the people kept their--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pantry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pantry. Yeah, pantry. Sorry. Pantry, and we saw compote, and bread, and butter, and meat, and salami.

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually it's a larder, is what they used to call it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Larder, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Larder, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: People who started to eat larder, let's say, they get [INAUDIBLE] or anything. And many people died this time liberation. So I went to the-- I started to walking. Magda, my friend--

speaker: interviewer
text: Magda, who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Magda [? Camanish, ?] who gave a testimony in Israel. We called her [? 'Crumpish' ?] Magda. Until nowadays, we call her [? 'Crumpish' ?] Magda, because she was at the potato peeling girls. And she was whistling for them arias, opera, operetta. So we are still good friend, corresponding. We are going there. She's coming here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wanted to steal some food. So then I thought I need a wagon with horses. And the Russian kept about 20, 50 German on their wagon. They were evacuating before the Russian, in the country, Germany, in the main land. So the Russians started to talk with me. And I know a few words. My village where I was born, people talked Slav. So all of the sudden, I saw he throwing out a man and a woman from the wagon. It was like a tent. Covered wagon, like a covered--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the top of the wagon, covered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told me, [NON-ENGLISH]. And he pulled me up to the wagon. He stand beside me, and he started to running with the horses with the wagon and with me. And I thought, oh, my god. We are going to Moscow. He is not going-- he doesn't know who I am, but he exactly went to this place where we were liberated, because he saw everybody was there. So we have a wagon. All the things, whatever you could name, was stored there, even [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't-- I don't know if it was jewelry there, or crystal, or whatever. The next thing what I know, my mother came on the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this? I'm sorry. This was-- what was the name of the place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Close to [? Ladenburg, ?] a city, somewhere over Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhere over Berlin. Who knows what was. And all of the sudden, I look in the back-- because we had horses at home. And when the man was sitting behind me, he sometimes gave me the horse--

speaker: interviewer
text: The reins.

speaker: interviewee
text: Reins. And for-- for-- from pleasure, I was doing it. So I was the big driver.

speaker: interviewee
text: So 17 woman were together on the wagon. They just jumped on. They didn't know what I am going to do. So I started to go. I was looking up where is the sun. It was lunch time. I know, because I saw the clock that time in the house where we were. So 12 o' clock, where is the sun? There is south. And I have to go to south, to Hungary. So I started. Don't ask the road. The-- yeah, I have two beautiful horse. The only trouble was I didn't know how to start and how to stop them. So one guy explained to me, if you say, brrr, they're going to stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Russian steal my horses. They gave me sometimes a small and a very big one, because I had two beautiful horses. And at night, when we stopped in a village, in a house, empty, nobody wanted to take care of the horses. So the Russian came and took away. So next day, I had to require two other horses.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- we cooked this time. First of all, it was lots of things. I don't know what was in the wagon. Whoever-- the girls was sitting there. All day, they were mining all things from there. But we didn't have any sense what is good and what is [? worthy ?] or anything. Anyway, I was occupied with the horses.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so we started to come down south, and south, and south. And sometimes, the Russian with the tanks pushed us in the ditch. And they-- they went away. But their officer stopped them. And they have to pull out us. I am telling you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were not afraid at that time? How--

speaker: interviewee
text: What is afraid?

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't even know the meaning of the word.

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- what is afraid? Afraid of what? When we went through, after that, it could become any wars? Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your mother? Same kind of person as you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She started always to keep me back. And the Russian soldier let me go out from my way. So [? 'Crumpish' ?] Magda was sitting the very back, on a bag of horse something, [NON-ENGLISH] they called.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oats?

speaker: interviewee
text: What the horse eat, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a very steep hill. So my two-- the horses went crazy. I didn't have brake. Nothing. We started to rushing down. She was falling down with the bag of the horse food. And I thought this is the end of the war, so I pulled the rein. And I thought, I am going to take the horses against a fence, so we will stop somehow. And the horses was running, still. And a soldier saw that I want to do, so they not stopped me. They not stopped me, but just said [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which 'run.'

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was the end of the hill down there. And they stopped the horses. They hold them. And I got crying. Crying, unstoppable, because I was responsible for everybody, right? And they were laughing and say, [NON-ENGLISH], which means, I understand why you crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, [? 'Crumpish' ?] Magda came after us, walking, because she fell down, and said, it's very-- not very nice. Your left me there. You were just running and running, and I was screaming after you. But no, you didn't want to hear me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we saw the first wagon-- the first train. Sorry. And we left everything there. The Polish people was standing like that, you know? They wanted everything. And we didn't know where the train goes. But the-- the machine was facing south, so we went on. Not in the train. It wasn't the train. It was tank trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were sitting outside under the big-- big tanks holding each other, not to fall down. And we just went by the train, and went, and went. And we came to-- through the Carpathian Mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Always sitting on the outside of the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always. We sometimes changed train, again, but facing south. So we arrived to home [INAUDIBLE]. We took all our lice coats, everything, dresses, put away. And they put it in the oven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some Jewish organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: What Jewish-- do you know who?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We get to know two Hungarian Jewish boy who was fighting with the British against German. So they directed us this time. [? 'Crumpish' ?] Magda, my mother, and me. Because the other three went on separate way, because they were sick. So the two sisters stayed [INAUDIBLE] my cousin. And we arrived home. We find my father in Miskolc. Our house was empty, no windows even, no furniture, nothing. So we went to live with friends in Miskolc. I got married in '45 with Erno Frank.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he also a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a survivor. He was home already in January. We were liberated March the 10. And April the 10th, I was with my mother in Hungary already, because I came by horse-pulled wagon. So we met our father. We didn't have my younger brother. We didn't know about my older brother. Later, in August, all of the sudden, he came home. But in August, 15, I got married already. We could-- we could collect some chickens and calf. So it was food for the wedding. So we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miskolc.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Miskolc.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a home? Or where? In a house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So tell me-- we're just gonna sum it up. Tell me about your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Both of them was born in Miskolc. My late husband was a-- on the police department against-- he was working-- he was a lawyer. So he was a consultant against the Hungarian [NON-ENGLISH] Arrow Cross people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Fascist-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --who was murderer, the gendarmes. He catch my two gendarmes who beat me up and my family. They got-- got life sentence. And we were over there [? or to ?] '56, when came the Hungarian Revolution. And Hungarian Revolution, they're talking-- I have a very different opinion, because my life was other-- also again, again, threatened. And I said, no way to stay here, because they are anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they made a so-called clean [INAUDIBLE], the Hungarian Revolution. But everybody came with backpack. What could they rob where? And they shoot. They broke in the windows, the store windows. And they put everything in the backpack. So for me, it wasn't-- and when they-- they're talking about freedom fight, I say, like hole in my head, freedom fight. So anyway, on '56-- my parents went in 1950 to Israel. But they were alone there, so they came to Detroit where my brother was. My brother-- brother left the country in '49 and came a very successful businessman in Detroit. So my parents came, because we couldn't leave the country. It was Communism there.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was not police at that time. We had a [NON-ENGLISH], a bakery. So we-- with passport, we came to Italy, from Italy to Vienna. And we waited over there. In '58, July, we arrived in Canada. We have a [INAUDIBLE], because my husband was 47 years old already. We had 11 years difference. So we find a nice life. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I want you just-- tell-- tell me the-- your daughters' names.

speaker: interviewee
text: My older daughter's name, Agnes. The younger one, Judy.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're both married?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're both married now. Judy has three kids. Agnes has--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did Judy marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judy married in 1970.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry Lazar, the son-in-law. And they have four kids, Michelle, married. Jeffrey, married. And Adina and Jeremy, Jeremy [? Erno, ?] named after my first husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughter, Agnes, is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Daughter, Agnes, is over there. I should start with here.

speaker: interviewer
text: It doesn't matter. Who's she married to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jack Deutsch. He's Hungarian also. They have Ronnie, Shira, married, and Naomi. They are very good kids. They have [NON-ENGLISH], which means [INAUDIBLE], respect for parents and grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened to your husband, your first husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was too late, and this time wasn't [INAUDIBLE] yet. So he died in 1983. My father died in 1965 in Detroit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1970, she came to live in Canada. She has her own apartment. And 11 years ago, she went to [INAUDIBLE]. God bless ups and downs. Everybody who ever put a brick in that institution. She will be two weeks later, 97 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: She has a memory loss, not Alzheimer, memory loss. And she has age disease. She's healthy in certain way, but she is weak. She's in the [? Baker's ?] Hospital now. And we have a lady companion seven days a week. And every single day, children, grandchildren, great-grandchildren-- she has 18 great-grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother has also two daughters. And they have 11 grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is how we are now.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're presently married to?

speaker: interviewee
text: And three days-- three years-- sorry, three days-- three years later, I married David Grossman. He was my butcher. So we know each other 25 years. We went to each other's [NON-ENGLISH], like bar mitzvah, wedding, always. And his wife died after my husband two years and five days. And we started to talk. And after his one year was over in January, we married in March.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also mentioned in the pre-interview that you're very active in the community working for Israel. Can you tell me what you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working always for Israel. I raised my children, my grandchildren. You know, we have only that. With my experience, I know we have Israel. We have to have Israel. I am working. I was president two different chapters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what-- what organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hadassah, [? Witzow ?]. My chapter is [INAUDIBLE] chapter. Her chapter was [? Hanasonesh ?] chapter, who was a Jewish girl, Hungarian, a hero. And I-- day an night, I am working for Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even now. I just made a [? parlor ?] meeting, October the 28th. We made a beautiful fund raising this time. I am very active. It is not active. I am very aggressive, OK? People is going on the other side when they saw me. And I-- I am going. I like if somebody is religious. It's no question about. I am also a Orthodox Jew. But for me, Jewishness is starting to support Israel, and how they support Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am for Israel. I went with my husband, David, in '89 to volunteer three weeks. And I tell you something. This was the best things in my life. I am ready even not to go. I wish they'd take me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just some last words. Any special thing you want to add, or anything you want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I like Canada. I am satisfied. I am every hour of the day, I remember back what happened with us. I am fighting against hate. I appreciate Canada, very much. I am a real patriot, I feel.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to visit back to Hungary only the cemeteries. I don't want to know them. When Jewish people said, oh, next summer, we are going home. Oh, are you going to Israel? They said, no, we are going to Hungary. I said, this is not your home. Any message for your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just ask them to be a good people, good Jewish people, good people for Israel. That is our future. Everything is there in that. Simple. [? Say-- ?] [? say ?] simple. Be a good people and help people. I always teach them to honor old people. The trees are green, [? HaShem, ?] God gave new dress for them. And when they came in, I was taking care of them. And when you came in, there is the cleaning lady. Hug her. She's a person also. Simplify things. I am a simple person, but I am a fighter.

speaker: interviewer
text: You sure are. Thank you, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. I appreciate for you. And for your video man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Really, he was very nice. This picture is my father and my mother. They get married 1919, December. And my father's name is Janu Krakovics. And my mother's maiden-- maiden name, Elizabeth Landsman. I am here, myself. I was three, four years old. And this picture was made in Miskolc.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got back this picture from the village people. All old picture was saved in a box.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1925. This picture of us made in Felsozsolca. That is our house, the backyard. And it was made in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is old picture also. And this is me, Lili Krakovics. This picture is heartbreaking. All four kids was killed in Auschwitz. In the back, my younger brother, Tibor, nickname, [? Tibbekeh. ?] It was made in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are they, and in what order, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: On his right is [? Wisejoshka. ?] On his left side is [? Ferdmalatsy ?]. We nicknamed him, [? 'pooh-pooh'. ?] And before there was Tibor younger brother, [? Vis Yoshka. ?] All of them died in Auschwitz with their parents. This picture was made 1945, August the 15 on Miskolc the [INAUDIBLE] Street Orthodox Shul. That was my wedding day with Erno Frank. Besides Erno is my parents, my mother, and my father, my cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother and father's names are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my father's name, Janu Krakovics and Elizabeth Landsman. And very left of me is my cousin. And on the right side, my lager friend, our famous five people line. And uncles and cousins there. This picture was made in Miskolc in 1953. This is Erno Frank, Lili Frank. In my lap is Agnes Frank. And Erno Frank's lap is Judy Frank. This picture was [INAUDIBLE] made in Canada already. That is Judy Frank. She married Henry Lazar in 1970. The parents of Judy is in her left side, Erno Frank and Lili Frank. On her right side, Elizabeth Krakovics, my mother, her grandmother. And our other daughter at the end of the right side, Agnes Frank. Now it is Agnes Deutsch. This is my daughter, Agnes Frank. Married Eugene Jack Deutsch 1972, August 27.

speaker: interviewer
text: In--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my treasure, my beautiful, smart, good, grandchildren. I am very happy being a grandmother. It is in Toronto a few years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are they? The names?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are-- they are Jeffrey, Ronnie, Naomi, Shira, Jeremy, Adina, and Michelle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which direction were you going to-- from when you say the names? Is that from left to right? Or right to left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started with Jeffrey in the white shirt.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
